title: Topic 146: Why Players Invest in NFT Games: Speculation Meets Gaming
content:
> **ABSTRACT**

> This paper delves into the curious case of non-fungible token (NFT) games, a digital frontier where the thrill of gaming collides with the speculative frenzy of a crypto gold rush. We explore the multifaceted motivations that compel players to invest significant time and, more importantly, money into these virtual worlds. Through a rigorous, albeit humorous, analysis of existing literature and real-world case studies, we dissect the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon. Our investigation reveals a diverse cast of characters, from the shrewd ‘trader-gamer’ to the aesthetically inclined ‘digital collector.’ We argue that NFT gaming is not merely a new genre but a fascinating social experiment, offering a window into the evolving nature of ownership, value, and human desire in the digital age. In essence, we ask: are these players pioneering a new economic paradigm or are they just playing the world’s most expensive game of digital dress-up? The answer, we suggest, is a little of both.

> **INTRODUCTION**

> Welcome, esteemed reader, to the bleeding edge of digital absurdity: the world of NFT gaming. It’s a realm where the familiar, comforting glow of a video game screen is now punctuated by the cold, hard reality of a fluctuating crypto wallet. For generations, the primary investment in video games was time, and perhaps the occasional, regrettable purchase of a garish virtual hat. Today, however, a new breed of gamer is emerging, one who views their digital escapades not just as a hobby, but as a high-stakes investment portfolio. They are the pioneers, the prospectors, the players who are betting the digital farm on the promise of non-fungible tokens.

> But what, precisely, is the allure? Why would a rational individual—or at least, someone who presents as rational—pour thousands of dollars into a digital asset that, to the uninitiated, looks suspiciously like a cartoon cat with a unique serial number? Is it the siren song of speculation, the dream of striking it rich in the digital gold rush? Or is there something deeper at play, a fundamental shift in our understanding of ownership and value? This paper will argue that the answer is a complex and often hilarious cocktail of both. We will journey into the heart of NFT gaming, exploring the psychological, economic, and social forces that are shaping this brave new world. We will meet the ‘trader-gamers,’ the digital deacons of decentralization, and the casual collectors who just really, *really* like their unique, blockchain-verified jpeg of a space hamster. So, grab your virtual pickaxe and a healthy dose of skepticism, as we dig into the fascinating, and frequently farcical, world of NFT gaming.

> **LITERATURE REVIEW**

> The academic world, ever the nimble gazelle in the fast-paced savanna of technological trends, has approached the phenomenon of NFT gaming with a mixture of cautious curiosity and outright bewilderment. Early attempts to grapple with the topic often read like a bewildered anthropologist’s field notes from a newly discovered, and deeply strange, digital tribe. However, a few brave scholars have ventured into this wilderness, and their findings, when pieced together, begin to form a coherent, if not slightly surreal, picture.

> A seminal, if you’ll pardon the pun, work in this nascent field is Ante's (2024) groundbreaking paper, “The polychotomy of NFT ownership: Motivational heterogeneity and underlying drivers.” [1] Ante, presumably after a long and soul-searching journey into the metaverse, identifies four distinct archetypes of NFT owners. First, we have the **Utilizers**, a pragmatic bunch who are in it for the functional benefits, whatever those may be. Then come the **Socializers**, the digital social butterflies who see NFTs as a ticket to an exclusive club, a way to network with fellow enthusiasts and, one assumes, compare the relative merits of their digital pet rocks. The third group, and the one that has attracted the most media attention, is the **Speculators**. These are the high-rollers, the digital prospectors, who view NFT games not as a source of entertainment, but as a volatile and potentially lucrative stock market. Finally, we have the **Aesthetes**, the art connoisseurs of the digital age, who are drawn to the artistic and cultural value of NFTs. They are the ones who will gaze lovingly at their unique, blockchain-verified image of a pixelated penguin, seeing not a collection of colored squares, but a masterpiece of our time.

> Building on this taxonomy of motivations, Reed (2022) in “The Psychology of Non-fungible Tokens” delves into the psychological quirks that make NFTs so compelling. [2] Reed points out that unlike traditional currency, which is as fungible as a politician’s principles, an NFT is a unique, one-of-a-kind digital artifact. This, Reed argues, creates a different psychological relationship with the object. It’s the difference between owning a dollar bill and owning *the* dollar bill that was used to pay for the Louisiana Purchase. This sense of unique ownership, combined with the well-established psychological principle of using tokens to shape behavior, creates a potent cocktail of desire and attachment. In essence, NFT games are a masterclass in behavioral psychology, a digital Skinner box where the rewards are not just points or power-ups, but potentially life-changing sums of cryptocurrency.

> Of course, no discussion of NFT gaming would be complete without mentioning the elephant in the room, or rather, the Axie in the virtual arena. The case of *Axie Infinity*, a game that at its peak boasted a player base larger than the population of some small countries, has become a focal point for researchers. A case study by Addressable.io (n.d.) astutely observes that the players of *Axie Infinity* are not just gamers; they are a new hybrid species: the ‘trader-gamer.’ [3] This finding has profound implications for game developers and researchers alike. It suggests that to understand the world of NFT gaming, we must look beyond the traditional frameworks of game design and player motivation. We are not just dealing with people who want to have fun; we are dealing with people who want to have fun *and* make a killing on the crypto market. This dual motivation, this blend of play and profit, is the defining characteristic of the NFT gaming landscape, and it is a theme that we will return to throughout this paper.

> **ANALYSIS**

> Now that we have waded through the scholarly marshlands of NFT gaming, it is time to roll up our sleeves and dissect the beast itself. What makes a person look at a digital image of a bored-looking ape and think, “Yes, I must have this, and I am willing to part with a sum of money that could purchase a modest-to-decent used car to acquire it”? The answer, dear reader, lies in a delightful cocktail of psychological biases, economic opportunism, and a healthy dose of good old-fashioned herd mentality.

> At the heart of the NFT gaming phenomenon is the seductive dance between gaming and gambling. For years, video games have been flirting with gambling mechanics, from the tantalizing allure of loot boxes to the addictive grind of gacha games. [4] NFT games, however, have taken this relationship to a whole new level. They have not just incorporated elements of gambling; they have made gambling the main event. The game is no longer just a game; it is a vibrant, and terrifyingly volatile, stock market. Every digital creature, every virtual plot of land, is a tradable asset, a speculative investment that could either skyrocket in value or plummet to the digital dustbin of history. This fusion of play and profit has given rise to the ‘trader-gamer,’ a fascinating hybrid who is as comfortable discussing the finer points of digital monster breeding as they are analyzing candlestick charts.

> The psychology of collecting also plays a crucial role in the NFT gaming world. Humans, as a species, have an innate desire to collect things. Whether it’s stamps, coins, or, in this case, digital cats, the act of acquiring and completing a set provides a profound sense of satisfaction and control. [5] NFT games tap into this deep-seated psychological need with ruthless efficiency. The scarcity of NFTs, the ability to provably own a unique digital item, and the thrill of the hunt all combine to create a powerful and addictive experience. It’s the Beanie Baby craze of the 1990s, but on the blockchain, and with the potential for much, much larger financial losses.

> Furthermore, we cannot ignore the social dynamics at play. As Ante’s research has shown, a significant portion of NFT owners are ‘Socializers,’ individuals who are drawn to the community and networking aspects of the scene. [1] Owning a particular NFT can be a status symbol, a digital key that unlocks access to an exclusive club. It’s the modern-day equivalent of owning a rare sports car or a piece of high-end art, but with the added bonus of being able to show it off to your online friends without having to leave the comfort of your gaming chair. This social element creates a powerful feedback loop, where the value of an NFT is not just determined by its utility or rarity, but by the strength and prestige of its community. In the world of NFT gaming, it’s not just what you own, but who you own it with.

> **REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES**

> To truly appreciate the beautiful absurdity of NFT gaming, we must venture out of the ivory tower of academia and into the wild, untamed frontier of the internet. Here, we find a treasure trove of real-world examples, cautionary tales, and stories of such staggering financial success that they will make you question your life choices.

> Our first stop is the poster child of NFT gaming, *Axie Infinity*. This game, which at its zenith was a multi-billion dollar behemoth, is a masterclass in the ‘play-to-earn’ model. Players would purchase a team of three digital creatures, called Axies, and then battle them against other players to earn a cryptocurrency called Smooth Love Potion (SLP). The SLP could then be sold on the open market, providing a real-world income for the players. For a time, it seemed like a utopian vision of the future of work, a world where people could earn a living by playing a video game. However, the dream soon turned into a nightmare. The value of SLP plummeted, and the game’s economy, which was heavily reliant on a constant influx of new players, began to crumble. The story of *Axie Infinity* is a sobering reminder of the inherent volatility of NFT gaming, a digital boom-and-bust cycle that would make even the most seasoned Wall Street trader’s head spin.

> Next, we have the curious case of *CryptoKitties*, the game that arguably started it all. Launched in 2017, *CryptoKitties* allowed players to buy, sell, and breed unique digital cats. The game quickly went viral, and at one point, the trading of these digital felines was responsible for a significant portion of all transactions on the Ethereum network. [6] People were spending tens, and even hundreds, of thousands of dollars on these cartoon cats. One particularly rare CryptoKitty, a digital cat named Dragon, sold for a staggering 600 ETH, which at the time was worth over $170,000. The *CryptoKitties* phenomenon was a perfect storm of novelty, speculation, and the human weakness for cute, collectible things. It was a glimpse into a future where the lines between a digital pet and a financial asset would become increasingly, and hilariously, blurred.

> These are just two examples from a vast and ever-expanding universe of NFT games. From virtual worlds where you can buy and sell digital real estate to card games where every card is a unique, tradable asset, the NFT gaming landscape is a testament to human ingenuity and our seemingly infinite capacity for both brilliant innovation and spectacular financial folly. It is a world where fortunes can be made and lost in the blink of an eye, and where the only thing more volatile than the market is the collective sanity of its participants.

> **DISCUSSION**

> So, what are we to make of this strange and wonderful world of NFT gaming? Is it a fleeting fad, a digital tulip mania for the 21st century? Or is it a glimpse into the future of entertainment, a paradigm shift that will forever change the way we play and interact with virtual worlds? The truth, as is often the case, likely lies somewhere in between.

> The rise of the ‘trader-gamer’ is perhaps the most significant and enduring legacy of the NFT gaming boom. The idea that a game can be both a source of entertainment and a viable financial investment is a powerful one, and it is a concept that is unlikely to disappear anytime soon. We are seeing a fundamental blurring of the lines between play and work, between leisure and labor. This has profound implications for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits. It suggests that we are moving towards a future where our digital lives and our financial lives are inextricably linked, where every click, every quest, and every virtual interaction has the potential to be a taxable event.

> The NFT gaming phenomenon also forces us to confront some uncomfortable questions about the nature of value and ownership. What does it mean to ‘own’ a digital object? Is a unique string of code on a blockchain really any different from a unique collection of atoms in the physical world? These are not just philosophical questions; they are legal and economic questions that will have a very real impact on our lives. As we spend more and more of our time in virtual worlds, the rules and regulations that govern these spaces will become increasingly important. The wild, unregulated frontier of NFT gaming may be a thing of the past, but the questions it has raised will continue to shape the future of the digital economy for years to come.

> **CONCLUSION**

> In conclusion, the world of NFT gaming is a fascinating, and often bewildering, place. It is a world where the thrill of the game is matched only by the thrill of the trade, and where the line between a digital pet and a financial portfolio has been well and truly blurred. We have seen how a potent cocktail of psychological biases, economic opportunism, and social dynamics has given rise to a new breed of gamer: the ‘trader-gamer.’ We have explored the cautionary tales of games like *Axie Infinity* and the bizarre, yet brilliant, success of *CryptoKitties*. And we have grappled with the profound questions that this new digital frontier has raised about the nature of value, ownership, and the future of work.

> So, are NFT games the future of entertainment or a get-rich-quick scheme dressed up in a clever disguise? The answer, it seems, is a resounding “yes.” It is a world of brilliant innovation and spectacular folly, of utopian dreams and dystopian realities. It is a world that is still very much in its infancy, and it is a world that is sure to provide us with plenty more entertainment, and plenty more research material, for years to come. One thing is for certain: the game has changed, and there’s no going back.
references:
Ante, L. (2024). The polychotomy of NFT ownership: Motivational heterogeneity and underlying drivers. *Digital Business*, *4*(2), 100091. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.digbus.2024.100091
Reed, P. (2022, May 17). The Psychology of Non-fungible Tokens. *Psychology Today*. Retrieved from https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/digital-world-real-world/202205/the-psychology-non-fungible-tokens
Addressable.io. (n.d.). *What Axie Infinity’s Data Reveals About Web3 Gamers*. Retrieved from https://www.addressable.io/case-study/axie-infinity
Molde, H., Holmøy, B., Merkesdal, A. G., Torsheim, T., & Pallesen, S. (2018). Are Video Games a Gateway to Gambling? A Longitudinal Study Based on a Representative Norwegian Sample. *Journal of Gambling Studies*, *35*(2), 605–619. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10899-018-9810-3
Kleine, J. (2021). Collectors: Personality between consumption and investment. *Journal of Behavioral and Experimental Economics*, *95*, 101770. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.socec.2021.101770
Jiang, X. J., & Liu, Y. H. (2021). CryptoKitties Transaction Network Analysis: The Rise and Fall of the First Blockchain Game Mania. *Frontiers in Physics*, *9*. https://doi.org/10.3389/fphy.2021.631665
